3. Preserving npm config registry resolution unless an explicit registry env var is set

2. The TypeScript type-check failed but the PR was merged anyway
3. There's something special about how `pr-preview.ts` works

Let me check how `pr-preview.ts` actually executes its effects - it might be using a different pattern than I expected.

input.title: fix(release): make explorer recon truthful
input.summary:
The PR correctly wires `NpmRegistry.NpmCli` into `explore()` to replace hardcoded placeholder values with real runtime observations. The core logic — `resolveNpmCredentials` absorbing `NpmCliError` gracefully, `resolveGitRemotes` using `Effect.option` for leniency, and propagating the new `NpmCli` requirement into `forecast.ts` and `pr.ts` — is sound. The `catchTag('GitError', ...)` fix in `resolveReleaseTarget` is a welcome bonus.

Two issues are worth flagging: the `toExecutorRuntimeConfig` test fixture now contains `registry: 'https://registry.npmjs.org'`, which `explore()` can never produce after this change (the type widening to `string | null` silently accepts it instead of forcing a fixture update). The `resolveGitRemotes` function swallows all `GitError`s via `Effect.option`, including genuine CLI failures — this is inconsistent with the fix made in the same PR to `resolveReleaseTarget`.

findings.title: toExecutorRuntimeConfig test fixture uses `registry: 'https://registry.npmjs.org'` — a value explore() can no longer produce
findings.body:
The `describe('toExecutorRuntimeConfig', ...)` block constructs a static `Recon` fixture with `npm: { ..., registry: 'https://registry.npmjs.org' }`. After this PR, `explore()` always returns `registry: null` when no `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` env var is set. TypeScript accepts the fixture because `string` satisfies the new `string | null` type — no compile error surfaces — but the fixture now silently tests a path that the live `explore()` function can no longer reach.

The fixture should be updated to `registry: null` (the new default), or a comment should explain it deliberately tests the explicit-registry variant. As written, it could mislead a future reader into thinking `'https://registry.npmjs.org'` is still the live default.

findings.title: `resolveGitRemotes` swallows all GitErrors via `Effect.option` — inconsistent with the `catchTag` fix in the same PR
findings.body:
```ts
const origin = yield* git.getRemoteUrl('origin').pipe(Effect.option)
```
`Effect.option` converts **any** `GitError` (CLI crash, permissions failure, not just 'no remote configured') into `Option.none()`, silently returning `{}`. This is inconsistent with the fix made in this very PR to `resolveReleaseTarget`, which changed from `Effect.catch((error: any) => ...)` to `Effect.catchTag('GitError', ...)` for precisely this reason.

The leniency is arguably intentional (remotes is informational; explore would already fail earlier if git is completely broken), but it's undocumented. A brief comment like `// Informational only — missing or unreachable origin is non-fatal` would prevent future readers from wondering whether this is intentional or an oversight.

residual_risks: The `NpmCredentials.registry` field changed from `string` to `string | null`. No current consumers of `recon.npm.registry` were found in the codebase, but downstream consumers outside this repo (or future code) that relied on the field always being a non-null URL will receive `null` silently — TypeScript won't catch this until they attempt to use the value as a `string`.

Duplicate `ChildProcessSpawner` service in `forecast.ts` layer composition (`spawnerLayer` appears both directly in `mergeAll` and baked into `npmLayer` via `Layer.provide(spawnerLayer)`). Effect deduplicates correctly, but this redundancy could cause confusion if `spawnerLayer` is ever swapped to a different implementation without updating the `npmLayer` binding.

content.text:
## Review Summary

**Verdict: findings** — two issues flagged, no blockers.

### What the PR does well

- **Graceful error absorption**: `resolveNpmCredentials` uses `Effect.result` to convert `NpmCliError` into a `{ authenticated: false }` shape instead of propagating the error. This is the right design — npm auth failure is non-fatal at recon time and should surface through lint rules, not a hard crash.
- **`catchTag` fix in `resolveReleaseTarget`**: Changing from `Effect.catch((error: any) => ...)` to `Effect.catchTag('GitError', ...)` is a solid correctness improvement — other error types from `getRemoteUrl` now propagate instead of being silently swallowed.
- **Test coverage**: Three distinct scenarios are tested (auth + remote populated, no registry env var → `null`, explicit registry env var → passed to `whoami`). The use of `Effect.die` for unused service methods (`pack`, `publish`) ensures unexpected calls are loud failures.
- **Layer wiring**: `pr-preview.ts` calling `explore()` is not a gap — `runPrPreview` is called from `pr.ts` which was updated to include `npmLayer`. The `NpmCli` requirement propagates correctly through Effect's `R` type parameter.

### Findings

| Priority | Issue |
|----------|-------|
| P2 | `toExecutorRuntimeConfig` test fixture at line 149 still asserts `registry: 'https://registry.npmjs.org'` — a value `explore()` can no longer produce. TypeScript silently accepts it (`string ⊆ string \| null`), making it a maintenance trap. |
| P3 | `resolveGitRemotes` uses `Effect.option` which swallows ALL `GitError`s (CLI failures, permission errors), not just "no remote configured". Intentionally lenient but undocumented — inconsistent with the `catchTag` fix in the same PR. |
